Penstemon is a genus of flowering plants in the plantain family (Plantaginaceae), native to the Americas. The health benefits of Penstemon are primarily related to its antiviral, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of respiratory infections and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Penstemon include iridoids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Penstemon include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory issues and promote immune function.

The genus Penstemon is part of the family Plantaginaceae, which includes plants such as plantains and foxgloves. This classification is based on the plant's morphology, anatomy, and genetic characteristics. The family Plantaginaceae is further classified within the order Lamiales, which includes plants with similar characteristics. Penstemon pseudoputus is native to the southwestern United States and Mexico.

The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may be beneficial for wound healing and skin irritations. Its medicinal uses are also thought to include soothing respiratory issues, such as coughs and sore throats, due to its expectorant properties.
